---
title: "GeoSampler — Step-by-Step User Guide"
subtitle: "A complete walkthrough of the Sentinel-2 Shiny Sampling Dashboard"
author: "Anish Bhattarai"
date: today
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    toc-title: "Contents"
    number-sections: true
    theme: cosmo
    code-fold: true
    smooth-scroll: true
execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
  message: false
---

# Introduction

GeoSampler is an R Shiny dashboard that guides you from **area definition → satellite imagery → statistically defensible GPS sample points** — all in your browser. This guide walks through every tab and control in the recommended order.

**Live URL:** <https://recuga.shinyapps.io/sentinel2_trial/>

**Recommended reading time:** 20–30 minutes for a first run; use the table of contents to jump to any tab.

---

# Before You Start

## What you need

| Item | Details |
|---|---|
| A web browser | Chrome or Firefox recommended |
| Your area boundary | GeoJSON, KML, KMZ, zipped Shapefile, or GeoPackage — or just draw on the map |
| Optional: imagery | If you have your own multispectral raster stack you can upload it instead of retrieving from Sentinel-2 |
| Optional: historical points | GeoJSON/vector file of prior field sample locations for comparison |

## Workflow overview

The dashboard follows a **left-to-right linear flow**. The Welcome tab displays a clickable workflow tree that mirrors this order:

```
Boundary → Variables & derivatives → Sampling → App vs prior → Cost → Report
```

You do not have to use every tab — at minimum you need **Boundary** and **Sampling → Generate sample points** for a working output.

---

# Tab 1 — Dashboard (Welcome)

When you open the app the **Dashboard** tab is shown first. It contains:

- A **workflow tree** — click any step to jump directly to that tab.
- A **Quick Start** card summarising the six-step flow.
- A **Sampling Methods** card explaining when to use each technique.
- A **Detailed Operating Guide** card with tips on AOI size, cloud constraints, layer QA, and output review.

::: {.callout-tip}
Hover over any tab title in the navigation bar for a one-line description of its purpose.
:::

**Nothing to fill in here — read the overview, then click the first workflow step or navigate to the Boundary tab.**

---

# Tab 2 — Boundary

## Purpose

Define one **Area of Interest (AOI)** polygon. Every subsequent tab — imagery retrieval, elevation, sampling, report — uses this boundary. Only one AOI is active at a time.

## Step-by-step

### Choose a boundary method

In the left sidebar, use the **Select Boundary Method** dropdown:

- **"I want to digitize"** — draw the boundary directly on the map.
- **"I have my own boundary layer"** — upload an existing vector file.

### Option A — Digitize on the map

1. Select **"I want to digitize"**.
2. Set your location one of two ways:
   - Click **Use My Location** (browser geolocation) — the map re-centres on your GPS position.
   - Or type a **Latitude** and **Longitude** and click **Set Location**.
3. On the map panel, use the drawing toolbar (top-left of the map):
   - Click the **polygon** or **rectangle** tool.
   - Click on the map to add vertices. Double-click to close the polygon.
   - Click **✓ Finish** in the toolbar, then **Done** to save the edits.
4. The boundary appears as a coloured overlay on the map.

::: {.callout-note}
**Editing tip:** If you need to adjust the shape, use the **Edit** tool in the drawing toolbar. After editing, click **Save** (not Done) in the toolbar before clicking the app's **Done** button.
:::

### Option B — Upload a file

1. Select **"I have my own boundary layer"**.
2. Click the file picker and select your boundary file.
   - Accepted: `.geojson`, `.json`, `.kml`, `.kmz`, `.zip` (zipped shapefile bundle), `.gpkg`.
   - **GeoJSON is preferred** — it is a single self-contained file.
   - For shapefiles, select all sidecar files (`.shp`, `.dbf`, `.shx`, `.prj`) together in one upload.
3. The boundary loads onto the map automatically.
4. Use the **X** button next to the uploader to clear and re-upload if needed.

### Review and export

- An **area summary** (hectares / km²) appears below the map once a valid boundary is loaded.
- Click **Download GeoJSON of Boundary** in the sidebar to save the AOI for reproducibility.
- Click **Next: Variables & derivatives** to move on.

::: {.callout-warning}
A boundary must be loaded before you can use the Sampling tab. If you navigate there without one, a red banner will remind you.
:::

---

# Tab 3 — Variables & Derivatives

This tab has **four sub-tabs**: Imagery, Elevation, Other layers, and Variable summary. Work through them in order.

## Sub-tab 3.1 — Imagery

### Purpose

Load a multispectral raster for your AOI. Vegetation indices (NDVI, NDRE, GNDVI, etc.) are computed automatically and become covariates for sampling.

### Imagery source options

Use the **Select Imagery Source** dropdown:

| Option | What it does |
|---|---|
| **Sentinel-2 (retrieve)** | Searches Microsoft Planetary Computer STAC for Sentinel-2 L2A scenes inside your AOI and date range |
| **Planet (retrieve)** | Retrieves Planet imagery (requires a Planet API key) |
| **Upload raster stack** | Upload your own multiband GeoTIFF |
| **Upload individual bands** | Upload NIR, Red, Red-Edge, Blue, Green as separate single-band TIFFs |
| **Upload VI TIFF** | Upload a pre-computed vegetation index raster |

### Using Sentinel-2 (recommended free option)

1. Select **Sentinel-2 (retrieve)**.
2. Set a **date range** using the From / To date pickers.
   - Broader windows (e.g., one growing season) increase the chance of cloud-free scenes.
3. Set the **Maximum Cloud Cover (%)** slider — 10% is a good default.
4. Optionally, set **Max scenes for median** (default 10) — the composite is built from this many evenly-spaced scenes to reduce noise.
5. Click **Search** — the sidebar populates with a list of available scenes sorted oldest → newest.
6. Inspect the **NDRE timeseries chart** (main panel) to see vegetation variability over time.
7. Select a scene (or keep the auto-selected one) and click **Retrieve** to download and composite it.
8. A spinner appears while bands are downloaded. When it completes, the false-colour preview renders on the map.

::: {.callout-tip}
If the timeseries chart shows fewer scenes than expected, widen the date range or increase the cloud cover threshold.
:::

### Using Planet imagery

1. Select **Planet (retrieve)**.
2. Enter your **Planet API key** in the field provided.
3. Set the date range and cloud limit as above.
4. Click **Retrieve Planet Data**. Click **Stop** to cancel.

### Uploading your own raster

1. Select **Upload raster stack** (all bands in one multiband GeoTIFF).
2. Click the file picker, select your `.tif`, then click **Assign Bands** to map band numbers to NIR/Red/RedEdge/Blue/Green.
3. The app computes VIs and displays them on the map.

For individual bands: select **Upload individual bands**, upload each band separately, then click **Stack Individual Bands**.

### What the imagery panel shows

- **Map** — false-colour composite of the AOI.
- **NDRE timeseries** — mean NDRE per scene (or uploaded VI) over time.
- **Scene list** — all scenes found by the search with cloud-cover percentages.

---

## Sub-tab 3.2 — Elevation

### Purpose

Load a Digital Elevation Model (DEM) and optionally compute terrain derivatives: slope, aspect, Topographic Wetness Index (TWI), and Topographic Position Index (TPI). These become additional covariates for sampling.

### Step-by-step

1. Navigate to the **Elevation** sub-tab.
2. Choose a **data source**:
   - **Retrieve elevation** — downloads a DEM from the `elevatr` package (uses AWS Terrain Tiles, no account needed).
   - **Upload DEM GeoTIFF** — upload your own `.tif` DEM.
3. For retrieval: click **Retrieve Elevation Data**. A spinner appears; retrieval typically takes 10–60 seconds depending on AOI size.
4. Once loaded, the DEM preview renders on the map.
5. In the **Terrain derivatives** section (appears after loading), check the boxes for the derivatives you want, then click **Compute derivatives**.
   - All checked derivatives are added as separate covariate layers available to the Sampling tab.

::: {.callout-note}
TWI and TPI are particularly useful for predicting soil moisture patterns and relative terrain position, which often correlate with crop yield and soil carbon.
:::

---

## Sub-tab 3.3 — Other layers

### Purpose

Add any additional GeoTIFF predictors that are not imagery or elevation — for example, a soil texture map, a historical yield map, or an EC survey layer.

### Step-by-step

1. Navigate to the **Other layers** sub-tab.
2. Choose a **data source** from the dropdown.
3. Click the file picker and upload your `.tif`.
4. Click **Add Layer**. The layer is added to the covariate pool and appears on the map.
5. Repeat for as many additional layers as needed.
6. Click **Clear All** to remove all added layers.

---

## Sub-tab 3.4 — Variable Summary

### Purpose

QA table showing the min / max / mean / median of every loaded covariate **inside the AOI**. Use this to verify that your rasters have sensible value ranges before sampling.

### Step-by-step

1. Navigate to the **Variable summary** sub-tab.
2. Click **Compute / refresh summary**.
3. A table appears with one row per loaded layer.
4. Look for anomalous ranges (e.g., NDVI > 1.0, elevation = 0 over land) — these indicate mismatched projections or no-data issues. Go back to the relevant sub-tab and reload the layer if needed.

---

# Tab 4 — Sampling

This tab has three sub-tabs: **Technique comparison**, **Generate sample points**, and **Population vs sample**.

## Sub-tab 4.1 — Technique comparison *(optional but recommended)*

### Purpose

Statistically benchmark six sampling strategies against your loaded covariates across a range of sample sizes. The output guides you to the most efficient method and the minimum sample count that achieves acceptable covariate coverage.

### Step-by-step

1. Navigate to **Sampling → Technique comparison**.
2. **Select covariates** — by default all loaded layers are checked. Uncheck layers that are not ecologically meaningful for your objective.
   - Use the **Covariate preset** dropdown for quick selections:
     - *Lean default* — fewer correlated VIs (recommended starting point)
     - *Quick scout* — NDVI + elevation only
     - *Fertility-focused* — NDVI, NDRE, GNDVI + TWI
     - *Full variability* — all layers
   - Click **Apply preset** to update the checkboxes.
3. **Recommend Zones** — click this button to run a k-means Within-Sum-of-Squares (WSS) elbow analysis. The app suggests an optimal zone count (k) shown on the WSS curve.
4. Set **Repeats per method per sample size** (default 3; increase to 5–8 for more stable results).
5. Click **Compare Sampling Techniques**. A progress indicator appears.
6. When complete, the **benchmark chart** shows covariate coverage score vs. sample size for each method.
7. Use the **threshold sliders** to define your acceptance criteria:
   - *Threshold target* — minimum % of the maximum score to consider "good enough".
   - *Minimum acceptable range coverage* — how well sample points must cover the full covariate range.
   - *Cost penalty weight* — balance between accuracy and cost (0 = pure accuracy, 1 = pure cost minimisation).
8. The app highlights the recommended **method + sample size** combination at the bottom.

::: {.callout-tip}
Run technique comparison before generating points. It prevents guessing — you see exactly which method needs fewest points to represent your field.
:::

---

## Sub-tab 4.2 — Generate sample points

### Purpose

Generate the final GPS sample point set based on your chosen method and covariate selection.

### Step-by-step

1. Navigate to **Sampling → Generate sample points**.
2. The covariate selection from Technique comparison carries over automatically (check or edit as needed).
3. Choose a **sampling method** from the dropdown:
   - Simple Random
   - Systematic Spread
   - Spread + cLHS
   - cLHS
   - Zone-based
   - Hybrid Zonal cLHS
4. Set **Number of Sample Points** — use the recommended value from Technique comparison if available.
5. For zone-based methods, the zone count (k) recommended by **Recommend Zones** is used automatically. You can override it.
6. For grid-based methods, set **Grid size (meters)** (minimum 6 m).
7. Set a **Negative Buffer** if you want to keep points away from the field edge (e.g., 10 m for headlands).
8. Set a **Random Seed** for reproducible results.
9. Click **Generate points** (or the equivalent button for your method).
10. Points appear on the map. Review their spatial distribution.
11. Use **Save Auto Point Edits** if you manually moved any points on the map.
12. Click **Download GeoJSON of sample points** to export the GPS coordinate file.

::: {.callout-note}
**Reproducibility:** record the Random Seed used. The same seed with the same covariates produces the same point set.
:::

---

## Sub-tab 4.3 — Population vs sample

### Purpose

Verify that your sample points represent the full covariate range of the AOI (the "population"). Shows density distributions of each covariate for all field cells vs. the sampled cells.

### Step-by-step

1. Navigate to **Population vs sample**.
2. Violin plots or density curves appear automatically once points are generated.
3. Check that the sample distribution (coloured) tracks the field distribution (grey) for each covariate. Major gaps indicate under-represented regions of covariate space.
4. If coverage looks poor, return to **Generate sample points**, increase the sample size or switch to cLHS, and re-generate.

---

# Tab 5 — App vs Prior

### Purpose

Compare your new app-generated sample locations against **historical field GPS points** collected in a previous season or by a prior protocol. Two views are available: a **map overlay** and **dual-violin covariate distributions**.

### Step-by-step

1. Navigate to the **App vs prior** tab.
2. In the sidebar, click the **historical points file picker** and upload your prior GPS points (same vector formats as boundary).
3. The **Map** tab updates automatically showing both old (historical, hollow circles) and new (app, filled circles) points.
4. Switch to the **Distributions** tab and click **Compute distributions**.
   - Dual-violin plots appear for each loaded covariate layer.
   - Compare the spread of historical vs. app samples in covariate space.
5. Use this comparison to justify your new sampling design in reports or publications — e.g., "the new design covers TWI ranges not represented in the prior grid."

::: {.callout-note}
Historical points must lie **inside the field boundary** and overlap the loaded covariate rasters, otherwise extraction will fail and a warning will appear.
:::

---

# Tab 6 — Cost

### Purpose

Estimate total sampling cost for your prior design vs. the app-recommended design, and visualise the savings.

### Step-by-step

1. Navigate to the **Cost** tab.
2. Fill in the sidebar inputs:
   - **Prior / grid sample count** — how many points you collected in the previous design (e.g., 40).
   - **App-recommended sample count** — pre-filled from Technique comparison if you used it; edit if needed.
   - **Cost per sample** — your actual cost per field visit in the chosen currency (lab fees, travel, time).
   - **Currency** — choose a preset (USD, EUR, GBP, CAD, AUD) or enter a custom label.
3. The **bar chart** updates automatically showing total cost for each design.
4. A **percent savings** summary appears below the chart.
5. These numbers are automatically included in the PDF report (Tab 7).

---

# Tab 7 — Report

### Purpose

Generate and download a one-page **PDF field-plan report** summarising your entire workflow run.

### What the PDF contains

- Summary section (AOI area, selected method, sample count, date)
- Map of the AOI with sample points
- Sample density distribution plot
- Population vs sample violin plots
- Historical vs app comparison (if prior points were uploaded)
- Cost comparison bar chart
- Technique comparison chart (if the comparison was run)

### Step-by-step

1. Navigate to the **Report** tab.
2. Review the **text preview** in the main panel to confirm the run details are correct.
3. Click **Download PDF report**.
4. The PDF saves to your downloads folder.

::: {.callout-tip}
The PDF export is deliberately capped in plot count to stay under the 1 GB memory limit on the free shinyapps.io tier. For the full coordinate table, export GeoJSON from the **Sampling** tab.
:::

---

# Tips and Troubleshooting

## App is slow / spinner does not stop

- Large AOIs dramatically increase retrieval and sampling runtime. If your field is > 500 ha, consider splitting into sub-fields.
- On the free shinyapps.io tier, only one CPU core is available. Complex cLHS with many covariates can take 1–3 minutes.
- Click the **Stop** button (red) next to any retrieval action to cancel and try with a smaller date range or fewer scenes.

## Boundary does not appear after upload

- Confirm the file is a **polygon** layer (not point or line).
- Multipolygons are accepted; the first feature is used.
- KMZ files must be unzipped or uploaded directly — the app handles KMZ internally.

## Sentinel-2 search returns 0 scenes

- Widen the date range (try 6–12 months).
- Increase the cloud cover threshold (try 30–50%).
- Confirm your AOI intersects a Sentinel-2 tile footprint (very small or polar AOIs may have sparse coverage).

## cLHS fails or returns fewer points than requested

- Ensure at least one raster layer is loaded and has coverage inside the AOI.
- Very small AOIs with coarse-resolution rasters may not have enough valid cells. Try a finer DEM or closer Sentinel-2 scene.

## Sampling points land outside the field

- Enable **Negative Buffer** (e.g., 10 m) on the Generate sample points sub-tab to pull points away from the boundary edge.

## Message log

A **message log** is accessible via the ≡ icon at the bottom-right of the app. It records all info messages and errors from the current session. Click **Download log** to save it for debugging.

---

# Exporting Results

| Output | Where to export |
|---|---|
| Boundary GeoJSON | **Boundary** tab → Download GeoJSON of Boundary |
| Sample points GeoJSON | **Sampling → Generate sample points** → Download GeoJSON |
| PDF field plan | **Report** tab → Download PDF report |
| Message log | Bottom-right ≡ → Download log |

---

# Running the Workflow as an R Script

If you prefer scripting over the GUI, `sentinel_workflow_functions.qmd` provides the same logic as standalone R functions. Render it to HTML for a browsable function reference:

```bash
quarto render sentinel_workflow_functions.qmd
```

Each section mirrors a tab:

- `read_boundary()` — Tab 2 (Boundary)
- `retrieve_sentinel()` / `compute_vi()` — Tab 3 (Imagery)
- `retrieve_elevation()` / `compute_terrain()` — Tab 3 (Elevation)
- `compare_sampling_methods()` — Tab 4.1 (Technique comparison)
- `generate_sample_points()` — Tab 4.2 (Generate points)

Function signatures are stable and can be moved into `R/*.R` to start an R package.

---

# Citation

If you use GeoSampler in your research please cite:

> Bhattarai, A. (2025). *GeoSampler: A Sentinel-2 Shiny Dashboard for Statistically Defensible Field Sampling*. GitHub. <https://github.com/Leoanish/geosampler>

---

*Guide version: 1.0 · Last updated: 2025*
